summary: O'Neil presents ten reasons to protest at Citigroup's annual shareholder meeting, organized by the Alternative Banking group of Occupy Wall Street. She traces Citigroup's role in dismantling Glass-Steagall, details their inadequately punished securities fraud, and highlights the revolving door between Citigroup and government. The post argues that Citigroup was the primary beneficiary of the 2008 bailout, continues to receive taxpayer subsidies, and spends tens of millions lobbying for further favorable treatment. O'Neil frames Citigroup as emblematic of a financial system where banks are too big to jail and politicians take orders from bankers. The piece serves as both a call to action and a concise indictment of Citigroup's outsized influence on American economic policy.
key_points: Glass-Steagall was repealed specifically to enable the Citibank-Travelers merger, and the banks merged before the act was even formally revoked
The SEC's proposed $285 million fine for Citigroup's securities fraud was less than half of the $600 million investors lost, and Citi was not required to admit wrongdoing
Citigroup's own internal 'Plutonomy Memos' openly strategized around increasing income inequality and investing in plutocracy
The entire 2008 bailout was arguably a cover for funneling money to Citigroup, which took $100 billion from the Fed
Citigroup exemplifies the revolving door between Wall Street and government, with figures like Robert Rubin and Jack Lew moving between Citi and the Treasury
money_quotes: Citi is one of those banks that is simply too big to jail.
They merged before the act was even revoked, giving us a great way to date the moment when politicians started taking orders from bankers.
The whole bailout was a cover for funneling money to Citi.
It is a good time to switch out of stocks that sell to the masses and back to the plutonomy basket.
When it collapsed, due in part to bank-friendly policies that Rubin advocated, he made more than $100 million while others lost everything.
Citi spent $62 million from 2001 to 2010 on lobbying in Washington. What's their return on that investment, do you think?
The bailout is still going on – and Citigroup is currently benefitting from the easy money that the Fed is offering, not to mention the $83 billion taxpayer subsidy. WTF?!
key_insight: Citigroup's history — from engineering Glass-Steagall's repeal to receiving the largest bailout to ongoing taxpayer subsidies — reveals a bank that has captured the political system to privatize gains and socialize losses on a massive scale.
